Entity_Description: Squamish is a municipality of 23,819 people (2021 Census) at the head of Howe Sound in British Columbia's Sea-to-Sky corridor, about 47 km north of Vancouver on Highway 99. A former forestry and railway town, it is now one of BC's fastest-growing municipalities, driven by outdoor recreation, tourism, and Vancouver commuters. The 700-metre granite face of the Stawamus Chief and nearby Shannon Falls dominate its landscape, while its housing mixes established neighbourhoods such as Brackendale and Valleycliffe with new family subdivisions.

Long_Description:
Squamish began as a logging and railway town at the head of Howe Sound, and for decades the mills and the rail yard set its pace. Today the pace is set by something else entirely: growth. One of BC's fastest-growing municipalities, Squamish has become the middle anchor of the Sea-to-Sky corridor — 47 kilometres from Vancouver down Highway 99, with Whistler up the road — and every year more commuters, remote workers, and outdoor athletes make it home. For a {BUSINESS_TYPE}, that means a market where new construction and established housing sit side by side, each with different {SERVICE} needs.

The geography here is unlike anywhere else in the province. The Stawamus Chief's granite face rises straight from the valley floor, Shannon Falls pours off the cliffs just south of town, and Howe Sound — a true fjord — funnels the winds Squamish is famous for up the valley. The climate is the mild, wet coastal end of the Sea-to-Sky gradient: long rainy seasons that test every building envelope, punctuated by wind events off the Sound. {COMPANY_NAME} plans {SERVICE} work in Squamish around that reality, because weather windows here are earned, not assumed.

The neighbourhoods tell the growth story. Brackendale, up-valley near the Squamish River, keeps an older, established character. Valleycliffe sits tucked beneath the Chief with mid-century roots. Garibaldi Highlands and Garibaldi Estates climb the benchlands with family homes, while downtown Squamish is densifying fast with new development near the waterfront. Each pocket presents a different {PROPERTY_TYPE} profile — from decades-old homes needing careful updating to brand-new builds where owners expect modern standards. {COMPANY_NAME} brings {YEARS_IN_BUSINESS} years of experience across exactly that mix, backed by a {RATING}-star reputation.

Coverage matters in a corridor town. From our Squamish service area, crews reach Britannia Beach in minutes down Highway 99, and Lions Bay within the half-hour, so the whole southern Sea-to-Sky benefits from the same {RESPONSE_TIME} response. {SERVICE_DESCRIPTION}

Squamish residents did not move here to spend weekends managing property problems — they came for the trails, the crags, and the Sound. Let {COMPANY_NAME} handle the {SERVICE} side. Request your {ESTIMATE_TYPE} today, or call {PHONE} to talk through your project with a team that actually knows the difference between a Brackendale lot and a downtown infill.
Local_Facts:
- Population: 23,819 (2021 Census), making Squamish one of BC's fastest-growing municipalities
- Located at the head of Howe Sound, about 47 km north of Vancouver on Highway 99 (the Sea-to-Sky Highway)
- The Stawamus Chief, a roughly 700-metre granite monolith, rises directly above town and is a world-renowned rock-climbing destination
- Shannon Falls, one of BC's tallest waterfalls, is just south of town off Highway 99
- Neighbourhoods include Brackendale, Valleycliffe, Garibaldi Highlands, Garibaldi Estates, and a densifying downtown
- Postal forward sortation area: V8B; part of the Squamish-Lillooet Regional District
- Economy has shifted from forestry and rail to outdoor recreation, tourism, and Vancouver commuters
